caveats: a: BLS occupation evidence is for NAICS 424, not six-digit beer wholesaling.
a: The software source describes capabilities and does not establish realized labor savings.
a: Exposure includes avoidable hiring and task substitution, not autonomous replacement of whole jobs.
rho: No representative beer-distributor implementation-rate study was located.
rho: Small LMM operators may have weaker data and integration capacity than vendor reference customers.
e: TTB describes permitted wholesale activity but does not measure the eligible share of LMM firms.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firm size.
s5: One public strategic acquisition is evidence of feasibility, not a representative transfer frequency.
s5: The cited transaction includes a broader beverage-alcohol distributor and may overrepresent large strategic deals.
q: TTB permitting supports structural intermediation but does not measure commercial benefit retention.
q: Retention varies materially with supplier concentration, retailer concentration and state franchise law.
d5: A single weak year may not represent the next five years.
d5: Craft-brewer statistics do not perfectly represent total beer handled by wholesalers.
d5: The ratio is intended to be constant-price and constant-quality; published dollar values were not used.
o: Federal permitting is not the same as a universal statutory mandate to use an independent wholesaler.
o: The estimate concerns an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not preservation of current staffing levels.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B product resale to licensed retailers and hospitality accounts; gross profit is principally the spread between acquisition cost and retailer invoice price, with routes, service frequency, territory and supplier-brand rights shaping economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Brewers that only distribute their own production, beverage retailers, pure import agents without wholesale operations, captive internal distribution units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers buying beer and ale for resale and repeatedly providing account sales, inventory holding, warehousing, route planning, delivery, merchandising, invoicing, and compliance to external retail and hospitality customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 424 nondurable-merchant-wholesaler workforce rather than beer wholesalers alone, while beverage-distribution software documents automatable workflows rather than wage-weighted substitution. I map exposure in sales support, order entry, forecasting, purchasing, routing, invoicing and administrative work onto that occupation mix, while treating driving, physical handling, relationship selling and field merchandising as mostly unexposed. Beer distributors may be more route- and handling-intensive than NAICS 424 overall, so the broad-industry proxy likely biases exposure upward.
caveats: BLS occupation evidence is for NAICS 424, not six-digit beer wholesaling.
The software source describes capabilities and does not establish realized labor savings.
Exposure includes avoidable hiring and task substitution, not autonomous replacement of whole jobs.
a.change_evidence: A current six-digit OEWS staffing-and-wage table for NAICS 424810 combined with task-level time studies from independent beer distributors and before/after headcount data from route-accounting or AI deployments.
a.rationale: The BLS industry page identifies truck drivers, freight/material movers, sales representatives and shipping/traffic clerks as common roles. Current beverage software spans account history, demand forecasting, inventory, pick-pack-deliver, routing and accounting, creating meaningful exposure in cognitive coordination and clerical work, but the physical delivery and shelf-execution core limits total wage-weighted substitution.

d5.bridge: Brewers Association data measures national beer production and imports, including channels and products not necessarily handled by independent merchant wholesalers, and reports a one-year 2025 volume change rather than five-year constant-quality wholesale demand. I use the observed 5.7% category contraction as a recent anchor, then moderate rather than mechanically compound that exceptional annual decline over five years. Direct brewery sales and channel shifts could make independent-distributor quantity decline faster than total beer volume, biasing this proxy upward.
caveats: A single weak year may not represent the next five years.
Craft-brewer statistics do not perfectly represent total beer handled by wholesalers.
The ratio is intended to be constant-price and constant-quality; published dollar values were not used.
d5.change_evidence: NIQ or Circana five-year channel-level beer case-volume forecasts, Beer Institute shipment series, and distributor depletion data separating independent wholesale from self-distribution and brewery-direct channels.
d5.rationale: Overall U.S. beer production and imports fell 5.7% in 2025, with craft production down 4%. Continued moderation in alcohol use and retailer rationalization argue for lower year-five physical demand, while category scale, portfolio adaptation and non-alcoholic or differentiated beer limit the case for collapse.

caveats: TTB describes permitted wholesale activity but does not measure the eligible share of LMM firms.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firm size.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of TTB wholesaler permittees matched to ownership, customer mix, service activity, EBITDA and related-party status, or a distributor census from state beer-wholesaler associations.
e.rationale: Most merchant wholesalers serve external retailers repeatedly and combine product resale with ongoing logistics and account service, matching the lens. The deduction allows for import-only entities, thin brokerage models, captive or producer-controlled operations, non-transferable shells, and businesses whose normalized EBITDA does not truly fall in the target band despite the dataset bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424810 ($8,070,056,395) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424810 ($82,343,083,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $715,193 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424810); class <5: 418 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,362,402 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $154,905 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,486,655 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $396,433 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 145 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,662,434 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $871,219 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 389 firms, avg revenue/firm $25,520,437 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,901,674 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 255 firms, avg revenue/firm $137,021,392 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,579,332 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $751,276,511 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $85,420,139 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 389

o.bridge: Federal TTB rules directly establish that alcohol wholesalers need permits and define resale to wholesalers or retailers, but they do not measure what share of future beer quantity must use an independent LMM operator. I translate the regulated wholesale role into high operator-required demand, discounting for lawful producer self-distribution, direct channels, larger distributor consolidation and software-enabled customer self-service. Because state exceptions vary, federal permit structure may bias the independent-operator share upward.
caveats: Federal permitting is not the same as a universal statutory mandate to use an independent wholesaler.
The estimate concerns an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not preservation of current staffing levels.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state shipment volumes by three-tier wholesaler, producer self-distribution and direct-to-consumer channel, plus legal tracking of self-distribution caps and retailer-direct reforms through 2031.
o.rationale: Physical beer still must be inventoried, compliantly sold and delivered, and TTB requires a basic permit before wholesale activity begins. Software can remove touches but cannot eliminate accountable custody, licensed selling, local delivery and exception handling; the larger substitution risk is consolidation or self-distribution, not a software-only service.

caveats: TTB permitting supports structural intermediation but does not measure commercial benefit retention.
Retention varies materially with supplier concentration, retailer concentration and state franchise law.
q.change_evidence: Distributor contracts and supplier agreements showing cost-plus provisions, price-reset mechanics, rebates, territory exclusivity and change-of-control clauses, plus gross-margin bridges after documented automation programs.
q.rationale: Product-spread revenue lets an efficient operator retain part of workflow savings without an explicit contractual pass-through, and territory or brand rights can dampen direct price competition. Over five years, powerful brewers and large retailers can renegotiate economics, competitors can price against lower costs, and customer renewals or bids can share the benefit, preventing full retention.

caveats: No representative beer-distributor implementation-rate study was located.
Small LMM operators may have weaker data and integration capacity than vendor reference customers.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor cohort data or distributor case studies reporting five-year adoption, workflow penetration, exception rates, implementation cost and FTE-hours saved by function, independently verified against payroll records.
rho.rationale: More than half of the exposed opportunity could be implemented in five years through mature forecasting, account intelligence, routing and workflow automation. Adoption is constrained by fragmented legacy data, supplier-specific systems, route exceptions, union or workforce change, customer-service requirements, and the need for human approval in regulated operations.

caveats: One public strategic acquisition is evidence of feasibility, not a representative transfer frequency.
The cited transaction includes a broader beverage-alcohol distributor and may overrepresent large strategic deals.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of verified beer-distributor control transfers divided by the annual active independent-firm population, supplemented by owner-age and succession surveys from state wholesaler associations and supplier consent outcomes.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability near one fifth reflects a mature, consolidating distributor population with observable strategic acquisitions, tempered by protected territories, family ownership, supplier approval requirements and the scarcity value that can encourage incumbents to hold. A large distributor's announced acquisition of the long-established Horizon Beverage operation confirms transferability but does not identify an industry rate.

principal_driver: Recurring route economics create a practical AI opportunity in forecasting, sales support and back-office coordination while licensed physical distribution remains necessary.
principal_weakness: Secular beer-volume pressure and a large physical labor core cap both growth and the wage-weighted share that AI can remove.

sources.supports: BLS identifies common wholesaler occupations including truck drivers, freight and material movers, sales representatives, and shipping, receiving and traffic clerks, anchoring the broad-industry occupation mix used for AI task exposure.
sources.title: Merchant Wholesalers, Nondurable Goods: NAICS 424

sources.supports: The fetched page describes beverage-distributor workflows spanning customer history, AI segmentation, demand forecasting, inventory, pick-pack-deliver, routing, compliance and accounting; used as capability evidence, not as proof of realized savings.
sources.title: The complete operating system for wine distribution

sources.supports: TTB states that alcohol wholesalers purchase for resale, sell to wholesalers or retailers, and must obtain a basic permit before commencing business, supporting the regulated operator role and lens definition.
sources.title: Wholesaler's Information

sources.supports: The page reports 2025 craft production down 4%, the overall beer category down 5.7% by volume, and craft retail dollar value down 2.8%, anchoring recent physical-demand direction.
sources.title: A Year of Correction for Craft Beer, With Early Signals of Recovery

sources.supports: The fetched announcement documents a planned control acquisition of a long-established New England beverage-alcohol distributor with beer, wine and spirits supplier relationships, supporting transfer feasibility but not an industry transfer rate.
sources.title: Southern Glazer's Wine & Spirits Announces Acquisition of Horizon Beverage Group, Inc.

title: Beer and Ale Merchant Wholesalers
